does: This paper investigates how large language models fail at register-transfer level hardware design tasks, introducing a structured error taxonomy that categorizes failures into syntactic, semantic, solvable functional, and unsolvable functional types. Through evaluation on the VerilogEval benchmark, the authors demonstrate that frontier models plateau at 90.8% initial pass rate, constrained primarily by unsolvable functional errors representing genuine knowledge gaps. The work reveals a critical surface convergence gap where optimization successfully eliminates syntax errors while simultaneously exacerbating deeper functional failures, showing that alignment techniques teach models to compile rather than develop true hardware design understanding. The research identifies that repeated sampling can address solvable errors but cannot overcome fundamental limitations in RTL coding capacity rooted in pretraining knowledge.
connects: This work builds on established research in evaluating language model capabilities on specialized code generation tasks and extends the growing literature examining LLM failures in formal systems like hardware design. It responds to prior work using VerilogEval and similar benchmarks that measure LLM performance on RTL coding, while drawing on cognitive science frameworks to understand failure modes more systematically. The paper engages with research on scaling laws and the limits of test-time compute, addressing claims about how sampling strategies and chain-of-thought methods scale model capabilities. It also connects to work examining the distinction between memorization versus genuine reasoning in neural networks, and builds on studies of alignment and fine-tuning techniques showing their limitations for domain-specific tasks.
enables: This error taxonomy and analysis framework enables more targeted diagnostics of language model capabilities in specialized domains beyond hardware design, supporting future research into what knowledge gaps truly limit model performance versus what limitations stem from optimization or decoding strategies. The empirical ceiling identified on hardware design suggests future work should investigate whether multimodal pretraining or domain-specific pretraining substantially shifts this boundary, or whether architectural changes are necessary. The surface convergence gap points toward developing improved training methods that preserve functional correctness while reducing syntax errors. The findings establish that evaluating progress in code generation requires deeper failure analysis than pass rate metrics, enabling better assessment of whether improvements represent genuine capability advances or superficial alignment phenomena.
